items.title: Genentech R&D layoffs hit top exec who’s been with the biotech nearly 30 years
items.snippet: The staffing changes will focus resources on work that creates the highest impact for patients, according to Genentech. The number of employees affected is unknown, but longtime veteran Vishva Dixit is out.

items.title: Teva terminates 250 jobs in Israel
items.snippet: Follow along as BioSpace tracks job cuts and restructuring initiatives.

items.title: Sky-high Parabilis, Kailera IPOs spur optimism—but only for derisked biotechs
items.snippet: Biotechs are benefitting from the AI tech frenzy and inflation, but validated pipelines and careful planning are still key to the recent record-setting IPOs, experts say.

items.title: Takeda’s AI-designed pill bests BMS’ Sotyktu in head-to-head psoriasis trial
items.snippet: Takeda eyes an FDA run for its investigational psoriasis pill after the drug elicited total skin clearance in more than 35% of patients at 16 weeks—more than 2.5 times that in controls taking Bristol Myers Squibb’s Sotyktu.

items.title: Kardigan stitches up $369M IPO plan
items.snippet: Analysts are cautiously optimistic about an IPO rebound for biopharma. BioSpace is keeping track of companies that seek to trade on the public markets this year.

items.title: SonoThera bags $125M series B to advance safer gene therapies
items.snippet: Instead of using viral vectors, SonoThera’s genetic medicines are delivered through an ultrasound-mediated technology that could help sidestep key safety issues with conventional delivery methods.

items.title: Novartis tightens bond with Orionis in up to $1.4B molecular glue deal
items.snippet: It’s the latest Big Pharma deal for molecular glue technology and Novartis’ second with Orionis Biosciences, after first linking up in March 2020. The backloaded agreement will see Novartis pay $40 million upfront.

items.title: Portugal, Spain and the new biotech frontier
items.snippet: In this episode of Denatured, you'll be hearing from Hannah Franklin, associate at Biovance Capital and Pablo Gabriel Cironi Lopez, director of life science investment at Caixa Capital Risc as they discuss the rise of Southern Europe’s biotech ecosystem.

items.title: The wait continues: pressure to operate lean among factors behind slow job market
items.snippet: Just 23% of respondents to a BioSpace LinkedIn poll expect the biopharma job market will rebound in 2026. Three experts discuss when they see a turnaround happening and how increasing emphasis on cost efficiency is impacting hiring practices.

items.title: May layoffs hit nearly 7,000, with most cuts from Takeda, BioNTech
items.snippet: For the second time in 2026, the number of biopharma professionals affected by made or projected workforce reductions rose year over year. In May, layoffs spiked nearly 50%, mainly due to Takeda and BioNTech axing a combined 6,360 employees.

items.title: Sanofi cans late-stage study for rare autoimmune disease on underwhelming efficacy
items.snippet: The discontinuation of Sanofi’s Phase 3 study for its complement inhibitor drug in chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy delivers an ‘obviously disappointing’ blow to the broader drug class, according to William Blair.

items.title: Disc’s FDA meeting sets ‘clear path’ for embattled rare blood disease drug
items.snippet: The FDA rejected bitopertin in February amid reports of skepticism from former CBER director Vinay Prasad, who has since departed the agency.

items.title: Update: Parabilis raises industry-record $670M IPO
items.snippet: Parabilis Medicines is joining the parade of biotechs going public, with one key difference—more money.

items.title: Lilly bets up to $1B+ with AlzeCure in search for next Alzheimer’s act
items.snippet: The star of the licensing agreement, a small-molecule gamma-secretase modulator, will help buff Eli Lilly’s position in Alzheimer’s disease, currently headlined by its anti-amyloid antibody Kisunla.

items.title: Lilly tees off with Novo at ADA, GSK’s $10.6B deal, FDA reform continues in Makary’s absence
items.snippet: Eli Lilly and obesity rival Novo Nordisk stole the show at the American Diabetes Association conference, though plenty of other companies also had data to show for their own weight loss assets; GSK strikes the biggest traditional pharma buyout of 2026; and FDA initiatives still lack clarity.

items.title: Local trade groups must support life sciences in these difficult times
items.snippet: As the industry faces policy changes and significant cuts to federal funding, local ecosystems can bolster companies through innovative resources to sustain growth and keep the U.S. at the center of biomedical innovation.

items.title: Lilly’s $25B+ M&A spree captures half of pharma’s 2026 capacity
items.snippet: Over the past decade, Eli Lilly has bought out more biotechs than any of the other top 12 pharmas by revenue—with 10 of those acquisitions arriving just this year.

items.title: New biotech group rises to fuel American rockets in China ‘space race’
items.snippet: Members of the American Biotech Innovation Alliance (ABIA) want to build a national biotech strategy—just like China did years ago.

items.title: Hikma outlines plan to invest $267M in Ohio plants, creating 350 jobs
items.snippet: The money will enable the addition of aseptic filling and oral solid dose capabilities to support Hikma’s products and contract manufacturing clients.

items.title: Four years and 219 lost companies later, biotech still has a cash problem
items.snippet: The public biotech universe has shrunk by more than 20% since 2021, yet financial stress remains entrenched across the sector, according to a report from EY Tuesday.

items.title: Lilly just bought renowned regulator Peter Marks a pipeline to play with
items.snippet: FDA veteran Peter Marks will now shape the future of Eli Lilly’s vaccines work after the buys of Curevo, LimmaTech Biologics and Vaccine Company for up to $3.8 billion total.

items.title: 3 top FDA, CDC roles filled by 15 different people in less than 18 months
items.snippet: The Department of Health and Human Services is spinning its wheels, unable to establish steady leadership at three major divisions—the CDC and the FDA’s two primary review units.

items.title: Unforced errors flummox HHS’ radical transparency bid—again
items.snippet: Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s health department has consistently touted radical transparency as being key to its mission. Recent instances—the FDA’s decision not to disclose the recipients of three Commissioner’s National Priority Vouchers and FDA and CDC choices not to publish vaccine-related papers—call this intent into q...

items.title: BioSpace elevates journalism as biopharma evolves in the Trump era
items.snippet: Neal and Azbee awards have validated our approach to reporting on the industry at a time of unprecedented shifts at the FDA and other federal agencies.
